I also telephoned our Club President who in turn reported the event to the FSDO. I was asked to contact another FSDO representative which I did. I explained the above description to him. He saw no violation and suggested that I file this report.After researching this issue I found that it is a known problem with the FAA's data broadcast from certain ADS-B towers.  Since ForeFlight does not overwrite or change any of the FAA data broadcasted it appeared on my ForeFlight map display as a TFR even though no TFR existed at any point that day. As I proceeded west I apparently began receiving data from different towers and the TFR disappeared from my map display. In hindsight I wish I would have taken a few screenshots of the towers that my Stratus was receiving so that I could give a more detailed description of the possible problem towers. In the interest of flight safety; this was a very unpleasant distraction that could possibly divert a pilot's attention from safe operation of the aircraft. I believe the FAA data broadcasted through the ADS-B towers needs to be evaluated and a solution to this issue found.  I would think that all of the ADB-B towers would be broadcasting the same data without erroneous data (i.e.; a non-existent TFR). This could possibly be a hardware/software issue.

After signing-in; I completed my self-brief. From the verbal briefings I received from the outgoing dispatchers; I remembered hearing that ZZZ Center would be ATC Zero for COVID cleaning. During my self-brief I saw ATC messages; and information in the written brief regarding the ATC Zero status for ZZZ Center. Because I knew my red-eye Flight ABCD; would not be affected by the ZZZ Center closure; I skimmed the messages; information in the briefing regarding the closure; and reroutes page; and did not notice that ZZZ1 Center was also going to be closed for a COVID cleaning tonight.  I planned the flight on a normal routing which traversed ZZZ1 Center. Approximately 30 minutes before departure; I realized that I had missed the fact that ZZZ1 Center was also going to be closed for COVID cleaning during the time in which my flight would traverse it. I asked the crew if they received their clearance yet. They did. I asked the Captain to call me so we could talk over the details of fuel/route. I called ATC to cancel the current filing; as well as called ZZZ airport station to send out the fueler to add more fuel to the aircraft. I computed a new route in SABRE that would avoid ZZZ1 Center; and re-released/refiled the flight to avoid ZZZ1 Center. I asked the crew to confirm the new routing with ATC. When ATC re-issued the clearance; they changed the departure routing. After confirming the new ATC route with the crew; I re-released the flight again.  The flight departed 6 minutes late.Expectation bias was a factor in me missing the ZZZ1 Center closure when originally planning the flight. Because I had remembered hearing about the ZZZ Center closure during turnover; I expected the messages and briefing information relating to ATC COVID cleaning to only contain information regarding the ZZZ Center closure. As I skimmed the messages; I expected ZZZ1 Center to read [as] ZZZ Center in the messages regarding the closures; and disregarded the messages too quickly; because I knew the ZZZ Center closure didn't affect my flight. Because I disregarded the messages; expecting it not to apply to my flight; I missed the fact that ZZZ1 Center was also impacted by the COVID cleanings; and that my flight would be need to be routed around ZZZ1 Center.

ILS Z XX was briefed by FO (First Officer). Discussed early configuration by ZZZZZ per 10-X page. Approach informed us GS (glideslope) was OTS (Out of Service). Changed to RNAV (GPS) X XX. Started getting vectors and approaching ETARE we received a GPWS warning. I had the terrain in sight and instructed FO to continue approach. Aircraft was in config 3; gear up...I believe;The [manual] pages do discuss the FOQA (Flight Operations Quality Assurance) issue at ZZZ. However; the note lists two intersections. ZZZZZ (ILS Z XX) and ZZZZZ1 (RNAV (GPS) X XY. At the time I thought maybe the courses were off enough that GPWS warnings only occurred on those approaches. Obviously; it applies to ALL approaches to XY&R.I think the two intersections on the 10-Y page FOQA Alert should be removed and should read something to the effect that the aircraft should be in final approach config at least 10 miles from TD (touchdown) and that it applies to ALL approaches.
Report_2_Narrative: Inbound to ZZZ I was the pilot flying. About one hour out we set up for the arrival and reviewed the applicable company charts. I believe that the airport was VFR but visibility was limited due to FU. We were expecting XX and so read the FOQA Alert and discussed the need to be configured By ZZZZZ1. As we got a closer we were given the RNAV X with vectors. We reconfigured the box and ran through the RNAV requirements. We were vectored east of Course cleared to 8400 ft. than once on the approach cleared to 7400 ft. (between ZZZZZ2 and ZZZZZ3). At 7400 ft. the path came alive about 2 miles from ZZZZZ3. ATC gave us a current altimeter that was .01 off of our display. I Believe we got the GPWS over ZZZZZ3. I immediately disconnected the auto pilot and began a climb. The captain said 'its because of our configuration'; he could see terrain and we should finish configuring and the approach. Landed successfully at ZZZ.On the FOQA alert page we discussed the need to be configured by ZZZZZ1 which is on XY (I later figured out). When I reviewed the approach for XX my eyes fell to ZZZZZ4 which is for XX. So I highlighted ZZZZZ4 on the approach so I would be configured for (what made sense) a 7000 ft. peak about 10 miles out. When we got the RNAV in my mind the restriction was lifted because we weren't flying over that peak and configured as usual. Ok so I knew I was going to get a FOQA call but didn't expect [report] so I'll write what I was planning on saying to FOQA. The 10-XY-X Page is confusing and limited. Confusing that XY and XX have similar sounding fixes. When you take someone like me with a short memory and say 'OK we need to be configured by ZZZZZ1. Exit out of 10-Y pages pull up the approach look for the fix......'Ah ZZZZZ1 there it is' I thought I was being compliant. Limited in the fact that those two fixes are the only ones mentioned and didn't cover the RNAV to XX. I believe we would have avoided the GPWS if the Emphasis items said a generic 'inbound be configured no later than 10 miles' I would have caught that. I realize that if I would have; upon reviewing the 10-Y page transposed the ZZZZZ fix to the RVAV its roughly ETAR. But we have to defer to the lowest common denominator (which is me). Thank you for your review.

Report_1_Narrative: We were doing the RNAV Visual to Runway 31. Weather was not a factor. Autopilot was on; everything was set; stabilized; on path; pretty much a perfect approach; or so we thought. VASI was also red over white; so from our end there was nothing wrong. Tower gave us a low altitude alert asking us to check our altimeter setting immediately. We had no GPWS (Ground Proximity Warning System) warning; like I said we were right on profile. My concern is; why did the Tower give us that alert? If we got that alert; shouldn't every Company aircraft flying the RNAV Visual to 31 get the same alert? Is there something wrong with the procedure? Their equipment? Or did we make a mistake? If we did; we don't know what we could have done differently. Could you guys look into this? Is LGA handing out this warning to everyone? Because if we received this alert based on what we were doing; then everyone landing on 31 on the RNAV should be in the same place altitude and position as us plus or minus 10 feet?

I was working both South and West sectors combined.  I was moderately busy working many VFR's; one or two IFRs; and two practice approaches.  The traffic was not overly complex; but I had to keep a good scan for traffic advisories due to many VFRs in the area.  Earlier I was even busier with more traffic resulting in a split of the airspace while losing our main frequency and dealing with several automated handoff problems.  We have fusion radar with 7 radars acquiring aircraft and I had at least a half dozen non ARTS (Automated Radar Terminal System) tracks resulting in adjacent airspace calling about 'handoff.'  There were also numerous wrong automated handoffs from adjacent airspace; i.e. aircraft should be handing off to S scope; but is in hand off status to D scope.  I had to slew and correct over a dozen bad handoffs.  I can only type and slew so fast (MF handoff OK S slew enter) while working a busier session.   That's how great the technology was working that day and may have led to the airspace violation.  Over on W airspace I saw Aircraft X with white coloration and a C tag indicating it would be handing off to me from CMH.  I grabbed the strip and noticed it was landing just outside W airspace in Toledo airspace.  I kept my scan going.  Numerous aircraft needed instructions on the S airspace.  By the time I got back to the Aircraft X it was 2 miles in my airspace flashing.  I accepted the handoff and descended the Aircraft X per the LOA.  A simple airspace violation by an IFR aircraft that I could have prevented.  I do not know if automated handoff was an issue on this; but it's pretty easy for a flashing white data block to catch your eye.  CMH may have had an issue on their end trying to get the aircraft to handoff to us.   Our facility has sent hundreds of reports about our automation issues. HUNDREDS.  We still get numerous wrong handoff status aircraft (flashing to D scope when it needs to flash to S scope).  We reconfigured the scopes twice on this day just to see if it was a configuration problem.  This was not an issue.  If we are to have this problem fixed then whoever we send these reports to; they and their team need [to] fix the computers and how they process our inbound aircraft to determine which scopes they go to.  We combined Akron Canton radar and Cleveland radar over a year ago and are still having problems.  Its a distraction that increases your workload.  Automation should make my job easier; right now; at CLE...it isn't.

There are way too many airfields in Central/South Florida that are in somewhat close proximity on the same CTAF (Common Traffic Advisory Frequency) frequency 123.05.   These airfields include GIF Winter Haven; 2IS Airglades; X04 Apopka; PCM Plant City; X26 Sebastian; and X39 Tampa North. On busy days usually towards the end of the week including Thur; Fri; Sat and Sun when the weather is good and many people are out flying; this frequency is completely jammed up much of the day with radio calls from all of the different airfields all at once. It is difficult sometimes to even get a quick call in. Some of these airfields to include GIF; PCM; X04 and X26 are very busy with flight school traffic; local GA (General Aviation) traffic and skydiving (at X26). This creates an unsafe situation when pilots cannot even hardly get a word in on CTAF to give a standard traffic call. Often times pilots will step on each other making calls which creates a jamming type sound which further makes comm more difficult. There shouldn't be this many airfields this close on the same frequency.

On final approach to Runway 20 at ICL there are grain bins in the flight path with the red obstacle [light] burned out.  I have requested for weeks that it be fixed; but has not as of today.  On a night landing training flight; student was making adjustment for a wind change and drifted too close to the bins because he could not see them without the red light.  I as the instructor had to take immediate action to avoid collision with them when I noticed that the wind had drifted us too close to them.  I will continue to request that the light be fixed and have students fly an above glide slope on Runway 20 in the future.

Our Lincoln transmitters are intermittent and randomly go unusable.  Sometimes at unacceptable and critical times. During this time I had about a dozen on my frequency; 124.0; when it just randomly became unusable.  I had 3 heavies in my IFR pattern for practice approaches.  I had an IFR jet inbound.  There were 2 VFRs inbound.  There was a VFR on a practice approach.  There was a VFR doing photo work over the lake area just west of the finals.  Plus additional satellite operations. It just suddenly wouldn't transmit.  I checked my headset.  Then I switched the plug into the second set of holes.  I tried using the foot pedal instead of my button.  I tried switching to the backup transmitters.  I tried guard frequency.  The Supervisor tried to use the PET [radio].  I switched to the override orange box and could reach 2 or 3 of them; but then only about 1 of the 5 tries would go through and the responses were broken; unreadable or very static-y.  We tried to put everyone on our backup 133.575; but it had the same issues.  We could hear pilots; but were unable to transmit anything back.  I told Tower to keep everyone on their frequency they had; and everyone was point out approved.  We frantically kept trying to reach the aircraft sitting on my frequency as they began to go through final and some began to get dangerously close to MVAs.  Finally; the combination of everything and the frequency seemed to snap back to working.In the next hour; we had probably 25% of the transmissions to aircraft not be received.  At many altitudes in areas that would typically work.  I used aircraft I could reach to relay to the ones I couldn't.  It was a nightmare. I may have had separation losses.  I think I kept altitude separation; but it was too busy and chaotic for myself or the Supervisor to work it and see. The VFR photo guy just decided on his own to head east; directly across the two finals in front of aircraft on [an] approach; without me telling him to; or without me being able to issue any traffic to anyone.  One of the heavies I had on a right base to RWY 18 at 040 took it upon himself just to turn in to the airport.  He ended up overflying at 040 before finally switching himself to tower frequency to enter their frequency.  This was the 2nd scariest thing I've ever had while working.  Not okay. The frequency transmitter issues need to be identified and fixed.  Just because they start working again; is not good enough.  We also need a real backup that we can use that transmits from a different source.  When our backup seems to go out at the exact same time; it is useless.  Our Omaha and Offutt frequency options are not in range for low Lincoln sector aircraft.For the last 2-3 weeks; something is wrong with the transmitting capabilities of our Lincoln sector frequencies.  Our primary 124.0 frequency will all of a sudden just not go out to the pilots.  We try switching to main/stand-by and it makes no difference.  We switch and do all the coordination with other facilities to use our backup frequency; 133.575; but then it has the same issue.  Sometimes we can use the Orange Voice Backup Override boxes; but it is also intermittent and when it goes out is maybe 2 by 5 at best with a lot of static and crackling.  If we try to work them in by leaving them on Omaha and/or Offutt frequencies; 120.1 or 124.95; the range isn't good enough to reach low aircraft on the south/west side of Lincoln and we lose contact that way completely. This is an urgent; serious issue.  When we called it into MOCC (Mid-States Operations Control Center) a week or two ago; the tech responded to us that 'it was probably due to the storm passing through' even though there was only light (level 1 and 2 precipitation).  When it came back online they just called it back in service without fixing anything or identifying the problem.  Yesterday it occurred while I had around a dozen aircraft on frequency.  The sudden loss of communications nearly caused major incidents and could have been catastrophic.  Luckily I had most of them altitude separated as an additional precaution at the time; but we still had a near MVA (Minimum Vectoring Altitude) bust as a result and may have lost separation in the chaos.  The Supervisor tried to use the PET; but our range doesn't reach Lincoln on them; and when it did intermittently go through; only stepped on my transmissions I was making through the override box and made it worse.

I work in a contract tower at EDC; Austin Executive. The tower cab does not have a field lighting panel nor the ability to turn on/off runway lights other than activating with a microphone. This allows the lights to stay on for 15 minutes. We may call the FBO to ask an operations person to turn the lights on but it may take several calls before we are successful.This evening it took three phone calls before I was successful in getting the lights manually turned on by the airport operations. In the meantime I had activated the lights using my microphone hoping that Ops would turn them on before the lights turned off. Aircraft X had just landed Runway 13 and exited the runway when the lights turned off. Had they timed out a minute before when they were in the landing phase it could have been an accident. I have also had the lights time out when a Cessna was taking the runway for departure roll on Runway 31. Again; had the lights timed out 10 seconds later it could have been horrible if they had been committed to departure roll and the lights turned off.I have many decades in both control towers and approach controls. I have never been in a control tower that does not have control of the lights. Airport management apparently does not want to pay for the install. I don't think they have factored in the amount of a lawsuit if; God forbid; there is a fatal accident because the lights turned off when an aircraft was in a critical phase of flight. Please help bring this to the attention of an authority that is able to address this situation before we have an accident.

I often fly late-day or evening; and routinely use the associated pilot-controlled airport lighting both to fix my position and for terminal guidance. Sky Manor has MIRL but does not have an airport beacon; so I often also turn on the lighting for Alexandria (N85); located approximately 2NM northwest; which has both MIRL and a rotating beacon. The frequency for this at Sky Manor is 122.975 (which doubles as the CTAF frequency); at Alexandria; 121.8 (with 122.975 as CTAF).On at least two separate occasions; coming from more than one direction; I have turned the airport lighting on for Alexandria and ended up momentarily distracting pilots and/or ATC by doing so. Alexandria is not very far away from Newark Liberty (EWR); or Lancaster (LNS); both of which use 121.8 as a Ground Control frequency and are apparently well within radio range and altitudes at and below 2000 ft MSL. Further research indicates that McGuire (WRI) may also be affected; as it's a similar distance as Newark.While I don't want to keep distracting others in the ATC system - and I've resolved the issue for myself by increasing familiarity with the airspace; which has reduced the necessity of me using the Alexandria airport beacon for guidance - I also realize that this has the capability to reduce my situational awareness; and at any event; aircraft intending to land at Alexandria at night must necessarily turn the airport lighting on; potentially distracting operations at the airports previously listed.I propose that the lighting frequency at Alexandria be changed; either to CTAF or to some other discrete frequency; with the intention of minimizing possible adverse operational implications for local controlled airports.

Landed in foggy conditions that got worse as we rolled down Runway 1 left. Tower told us to exit on Runway 31. The runway lights were not on and rolled past it. Then told to exit at Taxiway V and then take G; cross 31/13 and Bravo to the gate. There were no taxiway signs for Taxiway Golf that could be seen. Continued on Taxiway Victor and crossed Runway 31/13 at Victor instead of Golf. Tower then said just to proceed down Victor to Bravo and to the gate. No further problems. The visibility on the last half of Runway 1 Left kept getting worse and was probably less than 500 feet at times. [Caused by] fog and inadequate signage and no lights on the runway we were told to taxi off onto (13/31). Should have just asked for progressive taxi due to the worsening visibility.
Report_2_Narrative: After landing from a CAT II approach; we taxied off the runway. We were cleared to cross Runway 31 on G and we accidentally crossed on victor. When told to cross another active runway without any ground navigation aids to confirm which taxiway we were on; we believed we crossed the runway at G. We actually crossed the runway at V. The controller informed us after the crossing that we crossed on the wrong taxiway. Preceded to the gate without any incident. Visibility conditions got worse the further down the runway we taxied. The cause of the event was due to low visibility after landing from a CAT II. There weren't any signs to indicate the taxiway to which we were crossing a runway. Lights for the runway we were assigned to cross weren't on. The taxiway for which we exited the active led to a three way intersection to cross the runway for which we crossed at the wrong intersection. Ask for a progressive taxi when visibility conditions are low. Better signage on the airport. Lead off lights from the active runway to a taxiway and a marking on the ground for which taxiway we were on. Ground navigation on the iPad to show which taxiway we are on to use during times of low visibility.

Report_1_Narrative: When cleared for the visual approach for Runway 12L coming in to Saint Louis Lambert airport; I aimed for the numbers starting from the vector I was on. When I got to short final at a slight dog-legged angle I was instructed by Tower to go-around and join a left pattern for Runway 12L. After landing via the left pattern Tower informed me that a new system Saint Louis control was using had flagged my landing and the controller had no choice but to comply and give a go-around instruction. [Caused by] sensitivity of the new system being used by ATC. After the event I was instructed to submit this report in response. The system should be fixed as to not give errant flags. As for what I can do about it; I now recognize that the system is new and is prone to such errors so in the future I will make sure to square off a pseudo base leg in an attempt to avoid being flagged errantly.

Aircraft X received taxi instructions from gate 'taxi via TANGO 4; BRAVO.' The clearance was read back correctly.  While taxiing on TANGO; passing T2 intersection; FLL Ground said 'looks like you missed T2...taxi Tango 4 Bravo' (which was our original clearance.) An amended taxi clearance was apparently issued by ATC; but not heard or responded to by the crew of Aircraft X.  Since there is no longer a Ramp Controller working traffic; I have noticed that ATC communications are sporadic or are not being heard at all in the vicinity of the A/B Concourse; especially during pushback calls. [I] suggest ATC explore possible radio interference near the A/B Concourse.

A rotorcraft was in a all metal hanger with all doors closed except one with a 5 ft. walk through space. The aircraft was undergoing a FAR part 91. 411 413 static & transponder 24 month test. The aircraft is also ADS-B (aircraft dependent surveillance broadcast) equipped with a Lynx NGT9000 plus transponder (ads-b in and out). We had performed this test on numerous aircraft in the past in the same location within the building to contain the transponder altitude signal as the test aircraft altimeter system was raised to perform the required testing. No problems with outside aircraft either in the pattern for the airport or overflying the area were ever noted or reported. In the time period between the last aircraft test and this one; the airport had installed an additional ADS-B surveillance radar site. No notification was sent to any operators and we were not informed by the administrator. During the 91.411 / 413 testing; the new radar equipment received the ADS-B data and it was routed into the national airspace system automaticaly. The next day; we received a call from our FSDO (Flight Standards District Office) concerning a possible deviation by a commercial air carrier to a traffic conflict alert generated by the ADS-B system. The conditions were VFR but ATC advised the carrier of the presence of the test aircraft even though it was on the ground in a metal hanger.There should be a provision ; either through a phone contact with ATC to provide a ' test ' window for performing this type of aircraft maintenance with the registration number being posted on the ads-b data as an aircraft under test or other nomenclature. Possibly a software change to allow a maintenance function to be displayed through the ADS-B broadcast that would alert other aircraft that this particular ADS-B report is in fact not an airborne aircraft. Due to the sensitivity of these airport surveilance radars; it was found by phone calls to other operators; that this is a problem that is recurring around the country. The ramp test equipment has provisions to contain the transponder signal; but the effect of RF (Radio Frequency) leakage is not guaranteed.

A cell tower located about 1 mile northwest of my address has hazard red lights that have not functioned for about 2 weeks. I watch it from my bedroom window. It is located on the highest point in Aurora; and is obviously a safety hazard.

Visual Approach with both autopilots on; (CAT III showing) at approximately 3;000 feet aircraft excessively pitched nose down approximately 7-10 degrees following a false glide slope. Pilot flying (First Officer) disconnected the autopilot and leveled off at 2;000 feet as Tower called a low altitude alert. The aircraft glide slope recovered and showed a correct indication from the final approach fix to touchdown.  was written up and documented after shut down. Aircraft did not descend below 2000 feet prior to the final approach fix. Final Approach Fix altitude is 2;000 feet.

The Captain and I made a go around on our second attempt to landing. The first approach was discontinued due to RVR being reported below the minimums for the approach outside the final approach fix. Due to a glideslope anomaly on Runway 23. The glideslope a few hundred feet from reaching minimums made a quick movement towards half scale deflection down. The aircraft was on a constant rate of descent; airspeed and proper configuration; hand flown below 1700 feet per approach plate. At the point that I called runway in sight the Captain and I noticed that the aircraft was not going to touch down in the prescribed TDZ and accomplished the go around procedure. While being vectored for a 3rd attempt at landing; the Captain and I briefed that if the anomaly happened again; we would accomplished another go around. Before the 3rd approach; another flight was able to land on their first attempt. After requesting a PIREP from the crew; they said they were able to see the runway right at minimums. We were able to obtain visual reference with the runway right at minimums and complete a successful landing. In the hotel van with the other crew we asked them if they had issues with the glideslope and mentioned they did. They said it acted very similarly to our situation. It seems that this has been a recurring theme at CRW in the past as well. The cause of the second go-around was due to an inaccurate indication of the glideslope that put the crew in a situation where a landing would have been made outside of the TDZ. I think the FAA needs to take a deeper dive in what is causing glideslope anomalies for the ILS 23 into CRW. Especially coming into the winter months in mountainous areas where weather can deteriorate rapidly.

HNL ATIS [is] so long no one actually listens to it. For the last several years I have listened to HNL ATIS thousands of times. It is the worst and longest ATIS I have ever heard and the ultra-long; droning voice with redundant; useless information creates conditions under which a pilot can't listen to and understand the whole thing even if he tried. I have timed the ATIS broadcast several times and it's always over 2 minutes in length. This represents about 5 NM traveled in a small aircraft; and probably double that in an airliner. Additionally; most of the information is useless or suffers from poor quality control.Examples:-Listing all lighting inoperative for a runway that is closed; in the middle of the day.-Way too much time spent on taxiways out of service.-Duplication of information like 'LAHSO in effect' spoken twice.-'taxiway Relative Humidity out of service' referring to taxiway RH.-'men and equipment working all taxiways and runways' has always been on the ATIS.-'increased bird strike hazard all runways' has always been on the ATIS.Nobody listens to this. I have tried to pay attention to the whole thing and it's damn difficult even on the ground. Last year when there were more runway closures there were events where aircraft overran closed sections of runway and I believe ATIS was primarily to blame.Ideas to make this better:-Get a faster talking robot; or make a human record it (that will eliminate some errors).-Move information that's always on there (like the bird strike thing) to the chart supplement.-Consolidate groups of information; like 'Taxiways closed: B; D; F; RH; RB'.-Maybe make a separate frequency with a 'long ATIS' for out of towners who want to hear about how bad the bird strike hazard is getting.We have a 20 min leg from ZZZ. This ensures that 10% of the flight will be spent listening to HNL's ATIS. Please make the FAA fix this.

Crew was descending into ZZZ Airport at about [night] time. Weather revealed winds 350 with good visibility and MVFR conditions. With the higher winds out of the north; the crew elected to fly the RNP Approach which requires the PAPI to be operational to fly the approach. A review of the NOTAMs revealed this would not be an issue; though the REIL was out of service to this runway. Icing conditions on the approach prompted to crew to conduct a flap full landing with ice numbers. Additionally; Tower mentioned that they would be closing just prior to our arrival. The Captain; myself; briefed the autothrottles would be disconnected on the arrival; with the autopilot off once established inbound. I disconnected the autothrottles and left the autopilot on as briefed. We continued to configure through the landing checklist and saw the runway on the approach. Once we rounded out on final; I disconnected the autopilot and began slowing to our approach speed when the lights at the airport clicked off. I briefly shallowed the descent anticipating the possibility of a go-around and had my First Officer turn the lights to high which failed to bring the PAPI back online. Being VMC and within required company parameters for a safe approach; I inquired to my First Officer if he was comfortable with continuing under the conditions which he was. I corrected descent angle and the flight landed without any further incident.This was the flight crew's first time flying this approach; my first RNP approach in a while; and my fourth night landing in about 2.5 months. This RNP Approach requires the PAPI to be operational to use. With there being no PAPI NOTAM; it was determined that it would be appropriate to fly it. After we had landed we found that the Tower; attempting to enable the pilot controlled lighting; had inadvertently switched all lights off. This was corrected with my First Officer turning the lights back on but the action failed to turn the PAPI on. The ATIS had called for gusty winds out of the north but; once we were established on final; I noticed we had a quartering tailwind at 15 kt. which caused me some confusion. The light issue and shifting winds prompted me to shallow out the approach due to the possibility of a go-around. However; with the airport lights on minus the PAPI and in VMC conditions; I figured the safest course of action was to continue given the fact that we were within parameters required to continue the approach and land. My thinking was a go-around late at night; in mountainous terrain and icing conditions with a possible changing weather pattern; may not be the best idea given we were still within tailwind parameters and touchdown requirements for landing. To me; this incident illustrates how important it is to appropriately manage [non-forecasted] distractions; especially during high workload phases. Additionally; it's imperative that we strive to remain current even during prolonged periods of stagnation.When weather is in vicinity; ensure [that] the most accurate weather is obtained from Tower if necessary. Relay concerns of airport issues to ATC or go-around. Be wary of Tower closures near arrivals to airports. A reminder that higher elevation airports present unique challenges which must be reviewed and briefed prior to flying into them. A reminder to chair fly approaches to destination airports; especially if the approaches you intend to follow are unfamiliar. The importance of maintaining appropriate approach currency. Speed management and adequate monitoring during all flight phases. A reminder to fly the airplane first and; if mounting distractions cause confusion; go-around.
Report_2_Narrative: I briefed the descent and approach with Captain; Captain as pilot flying. Reviewed FMS approach briefing card. Verified all NOTAMs and approach requirements per the chart and current ATIS. Both First Officer and Captain acknowledged Runway End Identifier Lights were inoperative; [but was not] not required for approach clearance. Received approach clearance from Approach. Tower closed while just outside the FAF with acknowledgment from Tower on Tower frequency of no traffic in the area on frequency and no issues to report from the last reported ATIS. Broke out from IMC to VMC conditions before the FAF. Acknowledged 'runway' by First Officer and 'landing' by Captain. Clicked the CTAF frequency 5 times by [the] First Officer to assure lights would remain illuminated while on final; [as] requested by [the] Captain. Runway lights removed from view while just past the FAF.Then; [the] First Officer clicked 5 times on CTAF frequency again; after [the] Captain asked about the runway lights turning off; both runway lights illuminated again. Moments later we both acknowledged that we didn't see the PAPI to the left. CA (Captain) asked if I found the circumstances to land safe versus a missed approach late at night and snow in the forecast. [We] agreed it would be safe to land given the real time circumstances. Upon landing with no issue; Tower Controller got on CTAF frequency to apologize that they configured the runway lighting incorrectly; which is why it turned off momentarily. The First Officer reported the PAPI was also not working when lights returned. [The] ATC Controller reported on CTAF; 'That is weird;' and would be reporting it to airport operations. Great example in how reality and goal for safety require pilot judgment.

Report_1_Narrative: I was the Captain and Pilot Flying (PF) for Aircraft X from ZZZ to Richmond (RIC) on November XX; 2020; and observed the following. During cruise flight my First Officer and Pilot Monitoring (PM) alerted me that the last time (back in July) that she flew into RIC the airplane caught a false localizer signal and turned no where near the centerline of the respective runway. Having reviewed the Weather Briefing Package; I determined that RIC would be landing to the north; and elected to load the Runway 34 ILS approach. I confirmed all the waypoints with my PM and set up accordingly. I hard tuned the localizer frequency of 110.7; set the approach course of 337 degrees; and set the minimums of 370 feet on the Primary Flight Display (PFD). We received a clearance to cross the NEAVL intersection at 11;000 with a speed of 250 knots. We met that restriction; and then got a clearance to proceed to the KAFKA intersection and pilot's discretion to maintain 2;000 feet. That waypoint was loaded and confirmed. We began a descent. About 12 miles from the airport I slowed the aircraft to 210 knots and had selected Flaps 1. We were about 3;000 feet; and I slowed further to 180 knots; with a setting of Flaps 2. We were about 7 miles from the KAFKA waypoint; and confirmed the airport was insight. I began slowing the plane to 160 knots. About 3 miles from KAFKA; and the approach mode in the armed position; the airplane selected the LOC mode on the PFD and began to turn towards Runway 2. I turned the autopilot off; and manually continued to fly towards the KAFKA waypoint to lose more altitude. The airplane was about 2;300 feet; with 1;700 feet selected in the altitude selector. I continued the approach; and we landed safely. My First Officer questioned the Tower as to why this would happen. The Tower stated that both the Runway 34; and Runway 2 localizers were operational. He said that he would inform the staff at the airport.

Since the new Joplin Tower was built the beacon light was put on the new tower and as a result the visibility to see the light from a distance at night is very poor. I hangar at Joplin and have flown out of the airport for several years and can confirm the before and after the beacon light visibility has definitely been reduced. I have at times found it hard to locate the airport at night when flying into the airport; even using my GPS. I think the beacon light should be elevated higher up and made brighter.

During arrival to HAN; I was a jumpseat observer. We were at 2;000 ft. on the localizer; cleared for the ILS [Runway] 11 Left approach. Just outside the Final Approach Fix; I heard the Captain (Pilot Flying); say glideslope captured; and he set the missed approach altitude of 5;000 ft. in the altitude alerter. I was confused; because looking at the FO's (First Officer's) PFD (Primary Flight Display); I could see that we were below the glideslope; and it was no captured. I could not see the Captain's PFD to verify whether or not the glideslope was captured. The aircraft started descent from 2;000 ft.; even though we were not on the glideslope. I called out to check the glideslope and/or altitude. I did not have a hot mike; and maybe they weren't hearing me. The aircraft passed the Final Approach Fix approximately 500 ft. low. I called out the glideslope deviation again. The other jumpseat observer was also telling them to climb; and I yelled 'Go Around'. Then we got a GS caution with a line strike through the FMA on the PFD. At approximately 1;200 ft.; the go around was finally initiated with full scale deflection indicated on FO's PFD.During the surprise go around; we were instructed to maintain heading and climb to 5;000 ft. At some point the autopilot was disconnected; and situational awareness was deteriorating. The aircraft was veering to the right of track; 20 degrees nose up; and not following the flight director commands of 10 degrees nose up. I called out something like 'Who's flying the airplane; fly the airplane!' The flying pilot then steered back toward the flight director; regained control and the autopilot was re-engaged. After reaching 5;000 ft. we were vectored back for another approach to Runway 11 Left.Also of note; there was runway/taxiway construction at HAN airport that could have possibly created a glideslope anomaly. Though I could not see the Captain's flight instruments; it is possible there may have been a loss of glideslope indication at the start of the event. On the FO's PFD; I could see that the ILS was identified; and localizer/glideslope were displayed at all times.The Pilot Monitoring should have noticed on his instruments that the aircraft was not on glideslope; and should have called out the deviation immediately. When I saw the deviation; I was trying to alert the crew to fix the situation. I should have called out go around immediately.
Report_2_Narrative: While inbound on the localizer for RNWY (Runway) 11L; the glideslope was captured and the 'G/S' FMA was active. Shortly after capturing the glideslope; the 'G/S' FMA blinked and had a yellow strikethrough. The glideslope then started to fall beneath us; while the autopilot tried to increase the rate of descent. The Pilot Flying switched to V/S [Vertical Speed]; and rolled in more V/S down to stay on glideslope with a more than normal descent rate. I instructed to 'level off; level off' followed by 'climb back to 2;000 ft. It was then I noticed the glideslope dot had blinked and reappeared at two dots high; indicating we were low. The Captain in the observer seat then said 'Go Around; Go Around'. The Pilot Flying initiated the go around with the 'TOGA' [Takeoff Go-Around] switches and sometime thereafter turned off the autopilot. He then hand flew the go around to 5;000 ft. and present heading. After notifying Tower of our missed approach; we were told to contact Approach frequency. During this event; with my seat position; I was only able to observe the Captain's PFD [Primary Flight Display]. After landing safely and gathering all facts observed between crewmembers; it was apparent that the autopilot tried to capture a false glideslope and failed; thus giving us the yellow strikethrough the 'G/S' FMA."

Report_1_Narrative: Aircraft X was on the RNAV Z approach for Runway 22L. The aircraft had been cleared to land. The approach naturally brings aircraft in on a slight angle to the runway. Aircraft X appeared to be coming in at an angle on short final; but aligned with the runway prior to crossing the threshold. However; an ASDE alert triggered with Taxiway Y; which is parallel to Runway 22L. I instructed the aircraft to go-around. The aircraft went around and later landed without incident.This approach is likely to be problematic in the future because the angle it brings in aircraft on lines up with Taxiway Y until the last minute. It might be best this approach not be used if it can be avoided.

Landed Runway 35R and was instructed by DFW tower to taxi Q - Z hold short of Runway 35C. Approaching Runway 35C DFW Tower instructed us to cross 35C at Z landing traffic will land and hold short. As we began to proceed across Runway 35C; after crossing the hold short line; the Runway Entry Lights (REL's) illuminated red. Landing traffic was still airborne on approach to Runway 35C. Since we were already moving; I determined it would be safer to continue across the runway and exit rather than stop in the runway environment. Notified Tower Controller once clear of the Runway that the REL's illuminated as we crossed Runway 35C. The Controller apologized and stated that was not supposed to happen since they were in Land and Hold short (LAHSO) operations.Possible ground equipment failure or programming error.Not certain. Perhaps a question for DFW Tower.
Report_2_Narrative: Landed Runway 35R and was instructed by DFW Tower to 'taxi Q-Z; hold short of 35C.' Approaching 35C tower instructed us to cross 35C at Z; landing traffic will land and hold short. As we began to cross 35C; the runway entry lights illuminated red. Landing traffic was still airborne on approach and we were already across the hold short line; so we decided it was safest to continue and exit the runway. The Captain notified the Controller that the REL's had illuminated; and the controller apologized and said that shouldn't have happened.Possible equipment malfunction. I don't have any suggestions other than checking the operation of the equipment.

Report_1_Narrative: ZZZ Center queried us about our routing. Apparently they expected us to go further east due to ZZZ1 Center having a limited number of operating frequencies. Our filed routing showed us [on one routing and our clearance was different] based on what we heard and read back. ZZZ Center was under the impression that we had received an amendment to our flight plan. At our departure airport we were unable to get our clearance through ACARS and had to receive clearance on the radio. The flight continued without any further anxiety.ZZZ1 Center connections with their remote receivers and transmitter antennas were not operational; or so we were told. If we had been able to get our clearance on ACARS we could verify the clearance and that nothing was missed on either our part or the Controller's.
Report_2_Narrative: During cruise; ZZZ Center queried us about our routing. It seems they expected us on a different route further east due to ZZZ1 Center having communication issues. We double checked our FMC and the clearance that we both copied [earlier] since we couldn't get it through the ACARS; and it was clear what our route was. ZZZ ATC thought we had received an amendment but we never did. He explained ZZZ1's situation and what potentially happened; re-cleared us and agreed that nothing wrong on our side had occurred; just ATC misunderstanding. We continued our flight and landed without any further issues.

Report_1_Narrative: While on the visual approach into LEX Runway 4; inside the final approach fix and following the precision approach path indicator (PAPI) lights with two white and two red lights indicating on glide path; we started to feel we were low according to our actual sight picture. After referencing the electronic glide slope from the ILS we saw we were slightly more than half scale deflection low and corrected back onto the glide slope. We received a 'GLIDESLOPE' GPWS indication while correcting. When back on the glide slope we looked out at the PAPI again and saw it still showed two white and two red lights despite the change in glide path. The ILS chart for runway 4 does not mention that the glide slope and visual glide path are not coincident. I believe when we noticed we were low on the glide slope; the PAPI should have been indicating three reds and a white or maybe even four reds.There is something wrong with the alignment of the PAPI equipment for the runway. It should not have shown us to be two white and two red lights that low on the glide slope. The conditions were visual and easily fixed once we referenced the electronic glide slope fully.The Airport Operations Staff and the FAA need to conduct an alignment of the PAPI equipment. There could be a situation in low visibility or very dark conditions where an aircraft arrives at the minimum decision altitude; has the runway environment or approach lights in sight and relies on the PAPI to help keep the pilot's eyes outside to land; which could lead to an issue where they either see themselves as too high according to the PAPI (if it's aligned too low) or; if it only indicates two white two red at any altitude; may follow the PAPI to an unsafe landing either outside of the touchdown zone or short of the runway.

There was no adverse event or situation on this flight. I am making this report out of concern for safety and to hopefully prevent a future accident.Flying into PDK; Peachtree Airport in Atlanta; last night was typical - IFR approach in IMC using the best low approach procedure at the airport; the ILS to 21L. On final; breaking out of the overcast; my copilot and I did not see the runway because it was not lit despite our having triggered pilot controlled lighting on 120.0. We repeated the procedure and the runway lit up. Landing was uneventful.Lighting at this airport is poor. PDK is located in an urban area and is surrounded by ground lights not associated with the airport. One pilot; reportedly told ATC that all he could see was a 'sea of lights' when asked to call the airport in sight at night. I have had the same experience on multiple occasions. The pilot controlled lighting is erratic; and it is on a different frequency than CTAF after the Tower closes. The typical visual approach we get is from the side where it is hard to see the runway lights even when they are all on; and the beacon is weak and usually obscured by surrounding obstacles at the approach altitudes we are typically given. Moreover; the PAPIs have been NOTAMed out of service for quite some time now; so there is no visual vertical guidance at night.In my opinion; this setup is an accident waiting to happen; hence this report.

Aircraft X departed Runway 30 on an assigned 270 heading. Shortly after switching the aircraft to departure; I scanned the radar and saw Aircraft X turning through a 270 heading and continuing a left turn to the east directly into the arrival corridor for aircraft landing Runways 1L and 1C. At that moment; a controller at PCT; I think Departure Control contacted me. My initial thought was that Aircraft X had misunderstood my assigned heading and had not switched to Departure Control and was still on my frequency. While the controller was speaking to me other aircraft were talking on the frequency. The cross-talk prevented me from clearly understanding the information that was being relayed in the coordination. I heard the controller say climb the aircraft to 3;000 ft.; and turn him to the west immediately.Because I did not clearly hear the call sign of the aircraft he was referencing my mind jumped to Aircraft X as the aircraft needing the instructions. I immediately issued a climb and turn to Aircraft X and began issuing traffic alerts. Midway through issuing the traffic alerts I recognized that Aircraft X was descending and turning to join final for Runway 1L. In that instance I recognized what Departure Control was trying to coordinate and I sent Flight Number X around and vectored him away from Aircraft X and issued a traffic alert.Aircraft X checked onto my frequency on a mile final. I cleared him to land. Immediately after clearing him to land; the ASDE-X alerted with a taxiway proximity alert. It appeared to me that Aircraft X was correcting back toward Runway 1L. I asked the pilot if they requested priority handling. The pilot said no. The aircraft appeared aligned with the runway and the pilot confirmed they were; but because he was not a priority. I sent the plane around as required by the 7110.65. Coordination would have been easier and clearer had the radar data tag scratchpad entry for Aircraft X been changed to '01L' which would have indicated that he was inbound to land. The scratchpad showed the SID he filed; which contributed to my initial thinking that he was off course and not a priority. Additionally; had Departure Control requested priority handling in the coordination; I believe I would have immediately keyed onto the fact that Aircraft X was returning.The Airport Surface Detection Equipment (ASDE-X) ASDE Taxiway Arrival Prediction Software (ATAP) has had five alerts since its implementation on Date. In every case; the aircraft have been properly aligned with the runway and the alerts have been the result of poorly designed taxiway capture zones. The ATAP enhancement should be temporarily disabled and a full review of the ATAP system should occur.

While enroute on a flight plan including an ZZZZ segment; we had a complete data link failure; to include ACARS; CPDLC (Controller Pilot Data Link Communications); SATCOM; VHF DATA; HF DATA; and SATCOM voice. We received multiple EICAS messages along with status messages including CMU (Communications Management Unit). We attempted communication with the company via JETCOM; but we were out of range of available facilities. The [Relief Officer] attempted to connect to ARINC for a phone patch to company; but was unsuccessful. We were unable RNP-XX (Required Navigation Performance); a requirement for the upcoming airspace on our filed route. Another consideration was a SIGMET for severe turbulence along our route. We attempted to get an update through ATC; but were unable to. We attempted communication with ZZZ via HF; but could not contact them. I made the recommendation to continue until VHF communication with ZZZ was established giving us an opportunity to coordinate an RNP-XY route. The PIC (Pilot In Command) was not comfortable continuing and felt the most conservative course of action was a divert into Almaty. Almaty is an approved airport for our aircraft; however it was not on our flight plan and we had no communication with company to obtain an ARTR. The PIC and [Relief Pilot]; both with XX+ years each with the company; felt this was the prudent course of action. We continued as a crew and landed at Almaty without an amended flight release. Flight Operations Manual contains a note (for the [Aircraft] fleet) advising if no DATALINK; to attempt to continue and coordinate via VHF voice. It also says to reference fleet specific ORCA (Oceanic Route Clearance Communications) requirements. There was no guidance in the ORCA guide for this circumstance [for our aircraft]. Given the general difficulty of communication in that region; even with all systems operating normally; this would present an additional challenge. Further clarification in the fleet ORCA guide would be greatly beneficial. While likely not feasible; additional JETCOM coverage would have greatly helped as well.
Report_2_Narrative: Lost the following equipment: HF data L & R Satcom voice Satcom data Datalink system / lost CMU (Communications Management Unit). We lost datalink prior to the ZZZZ remote continental route. We were unable to obtain communications with any applicable air control facilities to update information on the reported severe turbulence in our paperwork or coordinate alternate RNPXY (Required Navigation Performance) routing north of the XXXX route so we diverted into ZZZZ1 prior to reaching the remote continental RNP-XX airspace.

Report_1_Narrative: When preflight planning; there is no way to accurately know which runway to expect at MHT. There is no digital ATIS. Weather for preflight planning is by a METAR.  This does give us the weather but not a runway.  Sometimes it is easy to know which runway to expect; but when the winds are light or calm there is no way of knowing. This would not be too much of a problem for many airports; but MHT uses complex RNAV arrivals with a branch point. We brief ahead of time. VHF ATIS is not in radio range at the normal time of our briefing. En route when the VHF ATIS is obtained it is sometimes necessary to change the runway which changes the arrival from the branch point. This is usually done while on the arrival in a high workload environment. Without the digital ATIS coupled with complex RNAV arrivals; we have the possibility of errors due to debriefing and reprogramming the FMC.

I flew into ACT to see our son. This was a night flight and upon entering the ACT airspace it was very difficult to see the runway lighting. The runway lights were very dim and indistinguishable from surrounding city lights. When I asked the tower to turn up the runway lights; the Tower Controller stated they haven't been able to control the runway lights for 9 months due to a lightning strike. They have filed numerous maintenance requests; but none have been acted upon. This is unacceptable and a safety of flight issue. I request the runway lights be fixed as soon as possible to allow the tower and pilots to control the lighting intensity.

Enroute at 2;000 feet when I received a message on GPS and EFIS saying RNAV signal is lost; ADS-B is lost. iPad does not show correct location. No problem because VMC and not going anywhere. No navigation and no traffic. I called Approach Control to ask if they knew of any GPS outages. They said they hadn't and nobody else was having a problem. After landing; about 20 minutes later the system was back to normal. Another pilot had the same experience. Called Approach Control again on the landline and they advised that they had gotten several reports but they had no prior record of it and they should have been advised that Navy was doing testing offshore. There were no local NOTAMs and when I went back to look; there was a NOTAM for the outage lasting for about a week at various times for Center. Send [a] NOTAM to all Approach Controls that underlie testing area and also to local airports so that flights that never touch overlying airspace are aware of possible outage. They should also be sent to EFB providers so they can include it in their system. Had this occurred in IMC it would have spiked the workload considerably and only VHF navigation would have worked.
